_meta.authoringNotes: Umbrella overview spanning venous, arterial, pulmonary, fat, air, amniotic, septic, and paradoxical embolism. Numbers cross-checked against 2019 ESC PE guideline, 2021 CHEST guideline on antithrombotic therapy, 2020 AHA scientific statement on PE, PEITHO and ULTIMA trials, and SEER/CDC epidemiology.

h1Title: Embolism: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Embolism is the sudden lodging of a travelling fragment of material — most often a blood clot, but also fat, air, septic vegetation, amniotic fluid, or tumour — inside an artery or vein, where it blocks downstream blood flow. Venous thromboembolism, the umbrella term for deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, affects roughly 900,000 Americans each year and kills 60,000-100,000 of them, making pulmonary embolism the third most common cardiovascular cause of death after heart attack and stroke. Arterial embolism to the brain causes about 1 in 6 ischaemic strokes; limb embolism threatens the leg within hours; fat embolism follows long-bone fracture; amniotic fluid embolism is a rare obstetric catastrophe. Outcomes depend on the embolus type, vessel size, and how quickly anticoagulation, thrombolysis, or embolectomy is started.

keyStats.prevalence: Venous thromboembolism: ~900,000 US cases/year (CDC, ~1-2 per 1,000 adults annually)
keyStats.demographics: Risk doubles with each decade after age 40; pregnancy raises VTE risk 4-5x; active cancer raises risk 4-7x
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of first pulmonary embolism is 62; fat embolism follows trauma in adults 20-50; amniotic fluid embolism affects pregnant patients aged 20-45
keyStats.globalCases: ~10 million venous thromboembolism events worldwide each year (ISTH 2014 estimate)

definition: Embolism (ICD-10: I82.90 for unspecified venous embolism and thrombosis; I26.- for pulmonary embolism; I74.- for arterial embolism) is the obstruction of a blood vessel by an embolus — any solid, liquid, or gaseous mass that has travelled in the bloodstream from a distant origin. The term spans several distinct entities that share a final pathology of acute vascular occlusion but differ in source material and clinical context. Thromboembolism is by far the most common form: a clot detaches from a deep vein (usually in the leg or pelvis) and lodges in the pulmonary arteries, or it forms in the left atrium during atrial fibrillation and embolizes to the brain or limb. Fat embolism follows long-bone fracture or orthopaedic surgery when marrow fat enters torn venous sinusoids. Air or gas embolism results from central venous catheter mishaps, decompression sickness in divers, or barotrauma. Septic embolism originates from infected heart valves in endocarditis. Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare anaphylactoid reaction to fetal material entering maternal circulation during labour. Paradoxical embolism occurs when a venous clot crosses a patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect into the systemic circulation, producing arterial events such as stroke in a young patient. Embolism is managed by vascular medicine, pulmonology, cardiology, hematology, obstetrics, or critical care depending on the type and territory affected.

typesClassification.description: Clot lodges in the pulmonary arteries after travelling from a deep vein, usually in the leg or pelvis. Presents with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, and hypoxia. Massive PE with shock carries 30-50% short-term mortality without reperfusion therapy.

Clot from the heart (atrial fibrillation, mural thrombus after myocardial infarction) lodges in a peripheral artery. Produces the six Ps — pain, pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, poikilothermia — and threatens limb viability within 4-6 hours.

Accounts for about 20% of ischaemic strokes overall and over half of cardioembolic strokes. Atrial fibrillation is the dominant source. Produces sudden focal neurological deficit; outcomes hinge on time to reperfusion with thrombolysis or thrombectomy.

Develops 24-72 hours after long-bone or pelvic fracture, intramedullary nailing, or marrow harvest. Triad of hypoxaemia, neurologic abnormality, and petechial rash over the chest, axillae, and conjunctivae. Incidence is 1-3% after isolated long-bone fracture.

Air enters venous or arterial circulation through central line manipulation, surgery, barotrauma, or diving decompression. As little as 50 mL of intravenous air can be fatal if it reaches the right ventricular outflow tract; arterial gas embolism affects coronary or cerebral circulation.

Infected emboli arise from bacterial vegetations on heart valves (infective endocarditis), infected vascular devices, or septic thrombophlebitis. Embolize to lung, brain, spleen, or kidney, producing infarction plus metastatic infection that requires both antibiotics and source control.

Rare obstetric emergency, incidence 1.9-7.7 per 100,000 deliveries. Sudden cardiovascular collapse, severe hypoxia, and disseminated intravascular coagulation during labour or within 30 minutes of delivery. Maternal mortality remains 20-60% despite modern critical care.

Venous clot crosses a right-to-left shunt — usually a patent foramen ovale, present in 25% of adults — and produces an arterial event such as stroke, limb ischaemia, or coronary occlusion. A frequent mechanism in cryptogenic stroke in patients under 60.

Fragments of cardiac myxoma, papillary fibroelastoma, or atherosclerotic plaque (cholesterol crystal embolism, often after angiography) lodge in distal arteries. Presents with livedo reticularis, blue toes, acute kidney injury, or focal organ infarction.

primarySymptoms: Sudden shortness of breath, often at rest and disproportionate to any preceding activity, present in roughly 73% of confirmed pulmonary embolism cases.
Pleuritic chest pain that sharpens on deep inspiration or coughing, more typical of smaller peripheral pulmonary emboli that infarct distal lung tissue.
Tachycardia above 100 beats per minute and unexplained tachypnoea above 20 breaths per minute, which together raise pretest probability of pulmonary embolism substantially.
Unilateral leg pain, swelling, warmth, or visible superficial veins suggesting an underlying deep vein thrombosis that may be the source of pulmonary or paradoxical embolism.
Sudden focal neurologic deficit — facial droop, arm weakness, slurred speech, visual loss, or aphasia — indicating embolic stroke and demanding immediate brain imaging.
Acute limb pain with pallor, coldness, absent distal pulses, and paraesthesia or weakness, the cardinal features of arterial limb embolism with limb at risk.
Haemoptysis, ranging from blood-streaked sputum to frank blood, occurring in roughly 10-15% of pulmonary embolism patients, particularly with peripheral infarction.
Syncope or near-syncope, especially with exertion, which signals a large central pulmonary embolism producing right ventricular outflow obstruction.
Petechial rash across the chest, axillae, neck, and conjunctivae appearing 24-72 hours after a long-bone fracture, the hallmark of fat embolism syndrome.
Sudden hypotension, cyanosis, and altered mental status in a labouring or recently delivered patient, raising concern for amniotic fluid embolism.
earlyWarningSigns: Calf tenderness, swelling, or a palpable cord along a deep vein after immobilisation, long-haul travel, surgery, or pregnancy
Mild persistent breathlessness or unexplained drop in exercise tolerance in the days following hospital discharge or a leg injury
Resting heart rate trending upward over 24-48 hours in a postoperative or post-fracture patient
Atrial fibrillation newly detected on a smartwatch, pulse check, or routine ECG — even brief episodes raise embolic stroke risk
Transient ischaemic attack with focal symptoms resolving within 24 hours — a warning shot for full embolic stroke within 90 days
emergencySigns: Sudden severe breathlessness with chest pain, fainting, or oxygen saturation below 90% — call emergency services immediately and request evaluation for pulmonary embolism
Acute unilateral leg coldness, pallor, and pulselessness — limb-threatening arterial embolism requires revascularisation within 6 hours
Sudden facial droop, arm weakness, or speech difficulty — possible embolic stroke; eligible patients can receive thrombolysis within 4.5 hours and thrombectomy within 24 hours
Cardiovascular collapse or seizure during labour or within 30 minutes of delivery — possible amniotic fluid embolism requiring immediate resuscitation
New petechial rash, hypoxia, and confusion 24-72 hours after a long-bone fracture or orthopaedic procedure — features of fat embolism syndrome

causes.description: The dominant mechanism of pulmonary embolism. A clot forms in a deep vein of the calf, thigh, or pelvis where Virchow's triad of stasis, endothelial injury, and hypercoagulability converge, then detaches and travels through the right heart into the pulmonary arteries.

causes.cause: Cardiogenic source (atrial fibrillation, valve disease, ventricular thrombus)
causes.description: Stagnant blood in a fibrillating left atrium, on a damaged or prosthetic valve, or in an akinetic left ventricle after myocardial infarction forms clot that embolises to the systemic circulation. Atrial fibrillation alone causes about 15% of all ischaemic strokes.

causes.cause: Long-bone or pelvic fracture and orthopaedic surgery
causes.description: Marrow fat globules and tissue thromboplastin enter torn venous sinusoids during fracture or intramedullary nailing. Reaming of the femoral or tibial canal forces marrow content into circulation, producing fat embolism syndrome in 1-3% of long-bone fractures.

Air enters circulation through open central venous catheters, surgical wounds above the level of the heart, hysteroscopic insufflation, or rapid ascent in diving. Volumes above 3-5 mL/kg air bolus can cause vapour lock in the right ventricle.

Bacterial or fungal vegetations on heart valves shed septic emboli to the lungs (right-sided endocarditis, often in people who inject drugs) or to the brain, spleen, kidneys, and limbs (left-sided endocarditis). Embolic events complicate 20-50% of cases.

Pregnancy raises venous thromboembolism risk 4-5-fold through hormonal hypercoagulability, venous compression, and reduced mobility. Amniotic fluid embolism arises when fetal squames and trophoblast enter maternal circulation through breached endocervical or placental vessels.

Cancer raises venous thromboembolism risk 4-7-fold through tissue factor expression, tumour invasion of vessels, and procoagulant therapies. Pancreatic, gastric, brain, and haematologic cancers carry the highest risk; the association is strongest in the first months after diagnosis.

Factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, antithrombin deficiency, protein C or S deficiency, and antiphospholipid syndrome each increase venous thromboembolism risk. Antiphospholipid syndrome also causes arterial events including stroke and limb ischaemia.

riskFactors.description: Venous thromboembolism incidence rises roughly doubling each decade after 40. By age 80 the annual incidence exceeds 1 per 100.

Hip and knee arthroplasty, abdominal cancer surgery, and major trauma carry 30-day VTE risks of 1-5% without prophylaxis. Standard practice now mandates pharmacologic and mechanical prophylaxis during admission.

Hospital stay over 3 days, plaster cast immobilisation, paralysis, or flights longer than 4 hours promote venous stasis. Travel-related VTE risk doubles with each additional 2 hours of seated immobility.
Increases VTE risk 4-7-fold. Adenocarcinomas of pancreas, stomach, brain, ovary, and lung carry the highest risk; the relative risk is greatest within the first 3 months of diagnosis.
Factor V Leiden (5% of Caucasians) raises VTE risk 5-7-fold heterozygous and over 50-fold homozygous. Antithrombin deficiency raises risk roughly 25-fold.
Non-valvular atrial fibrillation raises annual stroke risk to 1-15% depending on CHA2DS2-VASc score. Anticoagulation reduces relative risk by 64%.
Raises VTE risk 4-5x during pregnancy and up to 20x in the first 6 weeks postpartum. PE remains a leading direct cause of maternal mortality in high-income countries.

riskFactors.factor: Combined hormonal contraception and hormone therapy
riskFactors.description: Combined oral contraceptives raise VTE risk 2-6-fold depending on progestin type. Risk is highest in the first year and additive with smoking, obesity, and thrombophilia.
Doubles VTE risk; raises pulmonary embolism mortality through cardiopulmonary reserve limitation. Independent of other metabolic risk factors.
Recurrence reaches 30% at 10 years after a first unprovoked VTE off anticoagulation. Long-term anticoagulation reduces recurrence by over 80%.
Active smoking raises VTE risk roughly 1.5-fold and amplifies the risk associated with oestrogen-containing contraception and surgery.

affectedDemographics: Annual US incidence of venous thromboembolism: ~1-2 per 1,000 adults; African American adults have ~30-60% higher incidence than white adults
Pulmonary embolism is responsible for an estimated 60,000-100,000 US deaths each year (CDC)
Embolic stroke from atrial fibrillation affects roughly 1 in 5 ischaemic stroke patients; mean age over 70
Amniotic fluid embolism incidence is 1.9-7.7 per 100,000 deliveries with maternal mortality of 20-60%
Fat embolism syndrome complicates 1-3% of isolated long-bone fractures and up to 10% of polytrauma cases
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of embolism starts with rapid clinical risk stratification, then anatomic confirmation with the imaging test matched to the suspected territory. For pulmonary embolism, validated pretest probability scores (Wells, Geneva, PERC) categorize patients before any test is ordered. Low-probability patients with a normal age-adjusted D-dimer require no imaging. Higher-probability or D-dimer-positive patients proceed to computed tomographic pulmonary angiography (CTPA), the gold standard recommended by the 2019 European Society of Cardiology guideline, with sensitivity above 95% and specificity around 95% for clinically significant pulmonary embolism. Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scanning is preserved for pregnancy, severe contrast allergy, and renal failure. Bedside echocardiography assesses right ventricular strain and supports diagnosis in haemodynamically unstable patients who cannot travel to the scanner. For suspected deep vein thrombosis, compression duplex ultrasound is first-line, with sensitivity above 95% for proximal clot. For arterial limb embolism, urgent duplex ultrasound and CT angiography map the level of occlusion before surgery. Suspected embolic stroke requires non-contrast CT to exclude haemorrhage, followed by CT angiography of head and neck and, where available, perfusion imaging to define salvageable brain. Transesophageal echocardiography is the most sensitive test for left atrial appendage and valvular thrombus, vegetations of infective endocarditis, and paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect. Magnetic resonance angiography is useful for aortic embolic sources and renal artery occlusion. Diagnosis of fat embolism syndrome remains clinical, supported by Gurd's criteria; amniotic fluid embolism is also a clinical diagnosis confirmed by exclusion of obstetric haemorrhage and anaphylaxis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging for pulmonary embolism. Visualises filling defects in segmental and subsegmental pulmonary arteries, assesses right ventricular size for risk stratification, and identifies alternative diagnoses such as pneumonia or aortic dissection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scanner with iodinated contrast delivered through an arm vein. The breath-hold scan takes about 15 seconds. Results are typically available within 30-60 minutes in emergency departments. Patients with severe renal impairment or contrast allergy may need an alternative test.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Sensitive but non-specific marker of fibrin breakdown. A normal age-adjusted level in a low or intermediate pretest probability patient rules out pulmonary embolism and deep vein thrombosis without further imaging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw with same-day result. The cut-off rises by age in adults over 50 (age × 10 µg/L for fibrinogen-equivalent units). Cannot rule out PE in high pretest probability patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging for suspected deep vein thrombosis. Demonstrates non-compressible vein segments and visualises clot. Also used for upper-limb DVT and femoral vein assessment before mechanical thrombectomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A bedside ultrasound of the leg veins lasting 20-30 minutes. The probe is pressed along the femoral, popliteal, and calf veins while the patient lies supine. No radiation, no contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Most sensitive test for left atrial appendage thrombus, valvular vegetations of infective endocarditis, intracardiac shunts including patent foramen ovale, and aortic atheroma. Guides cardioversion for atrial fibrillation and identifies paradoxical embolism sources.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is passed through the mouth into the oesophagus under sedation. The procedure takes 20-40 minutes and patients are observed during recovery. A sore throat the next day is common but transient.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Radiation-free vascular imaging useful in pregnancy and renal disease; characterises renal artery and aortic embolism, atypical PE in young patients, and silent brain infarcts from atheroembolism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An MRI scan with or without gadolinium contrast, lasting 30-60 minutes. Patients with non-MRI-compatible pacemakers or severe claustrophobia may need an alternative.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Alternative to CTPA in pregnancy, severe contrast allergy, or chronic kidney disease. Identifies perfusion defects without contrast. Most useful when chest X-ray is clear.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two-part nuclear medicine scan: inhaling a radiolabelled aerosol for ventilation imaging, then an injection of radiolabelled albumin for perfusion imaging. Total imaging time 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects atrial fibrillation and other cardiogenic sources of arterial embolism. In pulmonary embolism, may show sinus tachycardia, right axis deviation, T-wave inversion in V1-V4, or the S1Q3T3 pattern in massive cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Takes 5 minutes at the bedside. A normal ECG does not exclude PE; an abnormal ECG raises suspicion.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography of the affected arterial territory
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the level and extent of arterial occlusion in limb or cerebral embolism, identifies the embolic source where possible, and plans surgical embolectomy or endovascular thrombectomy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scan with iodinated contrast covering the relevant vascular bed. Takes 5-15 minutes and is widely available 24 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of embolism follows three goals: stabilise the patient, restore blood flow to the affected territory, and prevent recurrence. For acute pulmonary embolism, risk stratification using the 2019 ESC algorithm sorts patients into high-risk (haemodynamic instability), intermediate-high, intermediate-low, and low-risk groups. High-risk PE with shock or persistent hypotension receives systemic thrombolysis with alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours, or catheter-directed thrombolysis where bleeding risk is high, or surgical embolectomy when thrombolysis fails or is contraindicated. The PEITHO trial established that intermediate-risk PE benefits from full-dose tenecteplase only when haemodynamic decompensation develops, due to a significant bleeding penalty. The ULTIMA trial showed catheter-directed ultrasound-assisted thrombolysis improves right ventricular function over heparin alone, with lower bleeding than systemic lysis. The mainstay of treatment for all venous thromboembolism is anticoagulation. Initial therapy uses low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily) or a parenteral lead-in followed by transition to a direct oral anticoagulant — apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily, or rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days then 20 mg daily. DOACs are now preferred over warfarin for non-cancer VTE per ACCP CHEST 2021 guidance. Duration runs from 3 months for surgery-provoked VTE to indefinite for unprovoked, cancer-associated, or recurrent disease. Cancer-associated VTE is treated with low molecular weight heparin or apixaban or edoxaban, based on CARAVAGGIO and Hokusai-VTE Cancer trials. Inferior vena cava filters are reserved for patients with absolute contraindications to anticoagulation; retrievable devices should be removed once anticoagulation can be safely resumed. Arterial limb embolism is treated with intravenous heparin and either surgical embolectomy with a Fogarty balloon or catheter-directed thrombolysis, with revascularisation aimed for within 6 hours. Embolic stroke receives intravenous tenecteplase or alteplase within 4.5 hours and mechanical thrombectomy within 24 hours for large vessel occlusion. Septic embolism is treated with intravenous antibiotics plus surgical management of the source, often valve replacement. Amniotic fluid embolism requires intensive supportive care with vasopressors, mechanical ventilation, transfusion of blood products for DIC, and where available extracorporeal membrane oxygenation.

medicalTreatments.name: Low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg subcutaneous every 12 hours; or 1.5 mg/kg once daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard initial parenteral anticoagulation for acute venous thromboembolism. Preferred for cancer-associated thrombosis and in pregnancy. Dose-adjusted in renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces VTE recurrence at 3 months by roughly 60% compared with no anticoagulation; equivalent or superior to unfractionated heparin for initial treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban 10 mg twice daily for 7 days then 5 mg twice daily; rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days then 20 mg daily; dabigatran 150 mg twice daily after parenteral lead-in; edoxaban 60 mg daily after parenteral lead-in)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral anticoagulation for most venous thromboembolism and non-valvular atrial fibrillation. Fixed dosing, no routine monitoring, fewer drug interactions than warfarin. Avoid in mechanical valves, severe renal impairment, and antiphospholipid syndrome with triple positivity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to warfarin for VTE recurrence and stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation, with roughly 30-50% reduction in major bleeding (especially intracranial haemorrhage).

medicalTreatments.name: Warfarin (target INR 2.0-3.0 for VTE and most atrial fibrillation; 2.5-3.5 for mechanical mitral valves)
medicalTreatments.description: Vitamin K antagonist used when DOACs are contraindicated. Requires monitoring with INR every 1-4 weeks and dietary consistency. Remains the agent of choice in mechanical heart valves and triple-positive antiphospholipid syndrome.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces VTE recurrence by roughly 90% versus placebo; reduces stroke in atrial fibrillation by 64%.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours, or tenecteplase weight-adjusted bolus)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for high-risk pulmonary embolism with shock or persistent hypotension, and for embolic stroke within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. Major bleeding rate is 5-10% including 1-2% intracranial haemorrhage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality in massive PE from 30-50% to 10-20%; for stroke, every 15-minute reduction in treatment delay yields about 4% better functional outcome.

medicalTreatments.name: Catheter-directed thrombolysis (low-dose alteplase delivered via pulmonary or peripheral arterial catheter, often with ultrasound assistance)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for intermediate-high-risk PE with right ventricular dysfunction and for ileofemoral DVT to reduce post-thrombotic syndrome. Lower bleeding than systemic lysis at equivalent reperfusion in early trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ULTIMA trial: improvement in right ventricular to left ventricular ratio at 24 hours of 0.30 versus 0.03 with heparin alone (p < 0.001). Bleeding rate ~2%.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical embolectomy
medicalTreatments.description: Open removal of pulmonary embolus via median sternotomy and cardiopulmonary bypass, or peripheral arterial embolectomy with a Fogarty catheter for limb ischaemia. Reserved for thrombolysis failure or contraindication.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Mortality 10-25% in centres with experienced teams for massive PE; limb salvage above 90% when arterial embolectomy is performed within 6 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Mechanical thrombectomy (large-bore aspiration or stent-retriever devices)
medicalTreatments.description: Endovascular removal of clot. Used for large vessel occlusion stroke within 24 hours of onset (DAWN, DEFUSE-3 trials), and increasingly for intermediate-high-risk PE (FLARE, EXTRACT-PE registries).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In large vessel occlusion stroke, mechanical thrombectomy improves 90-day functional independence (mRS 0-2) from 19% to 49% over medical therapy alone (DAWN).

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical pulmonary embolectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Open removal of central pulmonary clot via median sternotomy with cardiopulmonary bypass. Considered when systemic thrombolysis fails, is contraindicated, or when an intracardiac thrombus is straddling a patent foramen ovale.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 30-day mortality 10-25% in experienced centres; outcomes are best when surgery occurs before persistent shock.

surgicalOptions.name: Inferior vena cava filter placement
surgicalOptions.description: A retrievable mesh device placed in the inferior vena cava through the jugular or femoral vein. Indicated in acute proximal venous thromboembolism with absolute contraindication to anticoagulation, or recurrent PE despite therapeutic anticoagulation. Retrieve within 3-6 months when anticoagulation can resume.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces PE within 12 days but does not reduce overall mortality; long-term filters increase recurrent DVT and filter complications.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical arterial embolectomy with Fogarty balloon catheter
surgicalOptions.description: Standard surgical revascularisation for acute limb ischaemia caused by embolism. A balloon catheter is passed beyond the clot through a small arteriotomy, inflated, and pulled back to extract the embolus.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Limb salvage above 90% when performed within 6 hours of symptom onset; mortality 10-20% reflects underlying cardiac disease in elderly patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Valve replacement for infective endocarditis with embolic complications
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for large vegetations (>10 mm) with recurrent embolism, persistent infection despite antibiotics, or severe valvular regurgitation with heart failure. Performed by cardiac surgery on cardiopulmonary bypass.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Operative mortality 5-15% in stable patients, higher in active sepsis; substantially reduces further embolic events.

surgicalOptions.name: Patent foramen ovale (PFO) closure
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous device closure for selected patients under 60 with cryptogenic embolic stroke and a moderate-to-large PFO with right-to-left shunt (RESPECT, CLOSE, REDUCE trials).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces recurrent stroke by roughly 45% over medical therapy alone over 5 years; small increase in atrial fibrillation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Graduated compression stockings for prevention of post-thrombotic syndrome
alternativeTreatments.description: Used after proximal deep vein thrombosis to relieve oedema and venous symptoms. Evidence on prevention of post-thrombotic syndrome is mixed (SOX trial neutral) but symptomatic benefit is well-supported.

alternativeTreatments.name: Early ambulation and mechanical prophylaxis
alternativeTreatments.description: Intermittent pneumatic compression and early mobilisation reduce hospital-acquired VTE in surgical and medical patients, and are used in patients with bleeding risk who cannot receive pharmacologic prophylaxis.

whySeeSpecialist: Acute embolism is a hospital-managed condition. Vascular medicine, pulmonology, hematology, cardiology, and interventional radiology should be involved when there is haemodynamic instability, right ventricular dysfunction, unprovoked or recurrent venous thromboembolism, cancer-associated thrombosis, paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale, suspected antiphospholipid syndrome, or pregnancy. Hematology referral is essential when thrombophilia testing is needed and to determine duration of anticoagulation after a first event.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for hospitals with 24-hour CT pulmonary angiography, an established pulmonary embolism response team (PERT), interventional radiology capable of catheter-directed thrombolysis and mechanical thrombectomy, and a cardiac surgery service for embolectomy. For outpatient anticoagulation management, choose a centre with a dedicated anticoagulation clinic or a hematologist experienced with VTE; for cardioembolic stroke prevention, an electrophysiology service that can perform left atrial appendage occlusion when anticoagulation is not tolerated.
preventionStrategies: Receive evidence-based VTE prophylaxis during any hospital admission for surgery, immobilising medical illness, or major trauma — low molecular weight heparin or DOACs reduce hospital-acquired VTE by 50-70%
Maintain anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation with CHA2DS2-VASc score of 2 or above — DOACs reduce embolic stroke risk by roughly 65% over no therapy
Move every 1-2 hours and stay hydrated during flights longer than 4 hours, especially with additional VTE risk factors — wear graduated compression stockings if previously affected
Discuss combined hormonal contraception risk if you have a personal or family history of VTE, factor V Leiden, or active smoking — progestin-only alternatives are usually safer
Optimise BMI to below 30, stop smoking, and treat hypertension and diabetes — each modifiable risk factor lowers both venous and arterial embolic risk
Receive timely orthopaedic care after fractures with appropriate fixation and prophylaxis to lower fat embolism and VTE risk
lifestyleModifications: Take anticoagulant medication at the same time each day and avoid missed doses — DOAC missed-dose protocols differ from warfarin, so confirm with your prescriber
Wear a medical alert bracelet or carry a card listing your anticoagulant, dose, and indication — vital for emergency departments and surgical teams
Use a soft toothbrush and an electric razor to reduce bleeding risk; report black stools, prolonged bleeding, or unexplained bruising promptly
Limit alcohol to 1-2 units per day on warfarin to keep INR stable; with DOACs, occasional moderate alcohol is acceptable
Avoid contact sports while on therapeutic anticoagulation; choose swimming, walking, and cycling instead
Carry a recent INR or anticoagulation note when travelling abroad; pause anticoagulation only with medical guidance before procedures

recommended: Adequate hydration — at least 1.5-2 litres of water daily unless restricted for other reasons
Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fish, nuts, and olive oil to support cardiovascular health
Steady consumption of vitamin K-containing greens (spinach, kale, broccoli) if on warfarin — consistency matters more than restriction
Lean protein and adequate iron to maintain haemoglobin during therapy
Foods high in omega-3 fatty acids such as salmon, mackerel, and walnuts (note: DOACs and warfarin remain the active anticoagulants — omega-3 is adjunctive)
Whole grains and legumes for sustained energy and fibre
avoid: Excessive grapefruit and pomelo when on rivaroxaban or apixaban — significant CYP3A4 interaction
St John's wort, which lowers DOAC and warfarin levels and raises recurrent embolism risk
Crash dieting and prolonged fasting, which can destabilise INR control on warfarin
Excessive alcohol, especially binge drinking, which raises bleeding risk and destabilises anticoagulation
Cranberry juice in large amounts on warfarin — modestly raises INR
Unverified herbal supplements, especially ginkgo, ginger, garlic in high dose, and dong quai — all increase bleeding risk

exerciseGuidelines: Resume light walking within 24-48 hours of starting anticoagulation for venous thromboembolism — early mobilisation is safe and reduces post-thrombotic symptoms. Return to gradual aerobic exercise (walking, cycling, swimming) within 1-2 weeks. Avoid contact sports and activities with fall or impact risk while therapeutically anticoagulated. Patients who have had a large pulmonary embolism may benefit from supervised pulmonary rehabilitation if breathlessness persists beyond 3 months.
dailyManagement: Take anticoagulants exactly as prescribed and at consistent times; set reminders for twice-daily DOACs
Monitor for early bleeding signs — gum bleeding, bruising, dark stools, blood in urine — and seek same-day review if these occur
Track persistent leg swelling, breathlessness, or chest pain and contact the clinical team promptly
Schedule follow-up at 3-6 months for duration-of-treatment decisions, especially for unprovoked VTE
Coordinate with surgical or dental teams 5-7 days before any procedure for an anticoagulation bridging plan
prognosis: Outcomes after embolism depend on the type, the burden, and the speed of treatment. With appropriate anticoagulation, 3-month mortality after pulmonary embolism falls from approximately 30% in the haemodynamically unstable group to under 2% in low-risk patients managed as outpatients (Hestia and PESI criteria). Recurrence after a first unprovoked venous thromboembolism is roughly 10% in the first year and 30% at 10 years off anticoagulation; long-term anticoagulation reduces this by over 80%. Embolic stroke from atrial fibrillation carries 30-day mortality of 20-25% without reperfusion, falling to 10-15% with timely thrombolysis or thrombectomy. Acute limb embolism with revascularisation within 6 hours has limb salvage above 90%, falling sharply with delay. Amniotic fluid embolism remains the most lethal form, with maternal mortality of 20-60% even in high-resource settings. Predictors of poor outcome include age above 75, cancer, persistent right ventricular dysfunction, troponin elevation, and delayed reperfusion. Long-term complications include post-thrombotic syndrome, chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) in 2-4% of PE survivors, and post-stroke disability.
recoveryTimeline: Acute symptoms of pulmonary embolism — breathlessness, chest pain, tachycardia — typically improve over 1-2 weeks on anticoagulation, with most patients returning to baseline activity within 4-6 weeks. Persistent breathlessness beyond 3 months should prompt evaluation for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension. Deep vein thrombosis swelling and pain improve gradually over 2-6 weeks; post-thrombotic syndrome can develop over 1-2 years. Embolic stroke recovery follows a 3-6 month trajectory with the steepest gains in the first 3 months; rehabilitation continues for 6-12 months. Arterial limb embolism after successful revascularisation usually allows return to walking within 2-4 weeks. Fat embolism syndrome typically resolves over 7-10 days with supportive care.
complications: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH) — occurs in 2-4% of pulmonary embolism survivors and presents with progressive breathlessness; treatable with pulmonary endarterectomy in selected patients
Post-thrombotic syndrome — chronic leg swelling, pain, skin changes, and venous ulceration affecting 20-50% of patients after proximal DVT
Recurrent venous thromboembolism — 10% in the first year and 30% at 10 years after a first unprovoked event off anticoagulation
Embolic stroke and long-term neurological disability — 1 in 5 atrial fibrillation patients suffers stroke without anticoagulation
Limb loss from delayed treatment of arterial embolism — amputation rates rise above 20% beyond 12 hours of ischaemia
Major bleeding from anticoagulation — annual rate roughly 1-3% with DOACs, higher with warfarin; intracranial haemorrhage rate 0.2-0.5% per year
Disseminated intravascular coagulation from amniotic fluid or septic embolism — requires blood product transfusion and source control

supportResources.name: CDC — Venous Thromboembolism (Blood Clots)
supportResources.description: Patient-facing US public health resource with statistics, risk factors, and prevention information.

supportResources.name: National Blood Clot Alliance — Stop the Clot
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy group with information, support communities, and clinician directories.

supportResources.name: European Society of Cardiology — 2019 Guidelines on Acute Pulmonary Embolism
supportResources.description: Open-access clinical guideline used by emergency departments and cardiology services worldwide.

supportResources.name: American Heart Association — Cardioembolic Stroke
supportResources.description: Patient education on atrial fibrillation, stroke prevention, and anticoagulant choices.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation (vs Embolism)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atrial fibrillation is the irregular heart rhythm that often produces cardiogenic emboli, not the embolism itself. A patient can have atrial fibrillation for years without an embolic event; conversely, embolism can occur from other sources such as deep vein thrombosis or endocarditis. The two diagnoses coexist in cardioembolic stroke.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia (vs Pulmonary Embolism)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia presents with fever, productive cough, and consolidation on chest X-ray; pulmonary embolism more often presents with sudden breathlessness, pleuritic pain, tachycardia, and a clear chest film. CT pulmonary angiography distinguishes the two when clinical features overlap.

confusedWithConditions.name: Heart Failure (vs Pulmonary Embolism)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Heart failure causes progressive breathlessness, orthopnoea, and bilateral peripheral oedema with elevated BNP and characteristic echocardiographic findings. Pulmonary embolism is typically sudden, unilateral leg swelling is more suggestive of DVT, and right ventricular strain on imaging points to acute PE rather than chronic cardiac failure.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cerebral Aneurysm (vs Embolic Stroke)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cerebral aneurysm rupture presents with sudden severe headache (thunderclap), often with neck stiffness and altered consciousness, and shows subarachnoid blood on CT. Embolic stroke produces focal neurological deficit without haemorrhage and shows arterial occlusion on CT angiography.

relatedConditions.name: Atrial Fibrillation
relatedConditions.relevance: Single most common cardiogenic source of embolic stroke and systemic arterial embolism; anticoagulation choice is shared across both conditions.

relatedConditions.name: Heart Failure
relatedConditions.relevance: Left ventricular dysfunction and akinetic segments after myocardial infarction generate intracardiac thrombus that can embolise; venous stasis from heart failure also raises DVT risk.

relatedConditions.name: Pneumonia
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent differential diagnosis for acute breathlessness with chest pain; pneumonia also raises hospital-acquired venous thromboembolism risk through immobilisation and inflammation.

relatedConditions.name: Post-Stroke Rehabilitation
relatedConditions.relevance: Functional recovery pathway after embolic stroke; addresses motor, speech, and cognitive deficits and includes secondary embolism prevention.

relatedConditions.name: Hodgkin Lymphoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Active haematologic malignancy substantially raises venous thromboembolism risk through tumour-related procoagulant activity and chemotherapy effects.

faqs.question: What is the difference between embolism and thrombosis?
faqs.answer: Thrombosis is a clot forming in place inside a vessel. Embolism occurs when a fragment of clot, fat, air, or other material breaks off and lodges in a distant vessel. Most pulmonary embolisms originate from a deep vein thrombosis in the leg or pelvis.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of pulmonary embolism?
faqs.answer: Sudden unexplained breathlessness, sharp chest pain that worsens with deep breaths, fast heart rate, coughing up blood, fainting, and unilateral leg swelling. Any combination needs urgent evaluation, especially after surgery, immobilisation, or long-haul travel.

faqs.question: How is pulmonary embolism diagnosed?
faqs.answer: CT pulmonary angiography is the gold-standard test, with over 95% sensitivity for clinically significant pulmonary embolism. In low-risk patients, a normal age-adjusted D-dimer rules it out without imaging. Ventilation-perfusion scan is used when CT contrast is contraindicated.

faqs.question: How is pulmonary embolism treated?
faqs.answer: Most are treated with anticoagulation, usually a direct oral anticoagulant such as apixaban or rivaroxaban. High-risk PE with shock receives thrombolysis with alteplase or surgical embolectomy. Duration ranges from 3 months to lifelong depending on cause.

faqs.question: Can flying cause an embolism?
faqs.answer: Flights of 4 hours or more double venous thromboembolism risk because immobility and lower cabin pressure slow leg blood flow. Walking every 1-2 hours, staying hydrated, and wearing compression stockings reduce risk. People with prior VTE should discuss prophylaxis first.

faqs.question: What causes fat embolism syndrome?
faqs.answer: Fat embolism syndrome typically follows long-bone or pelvic fracture, intramedullary nailing, or marrow harvest, when marrow fat enters torn venous sinusoids. It develops 24-72 hours later with the triad of hypoxia, confusion, and a petechial rash across the chest and conjunctivae.

faqs.question: How dangerous is an air embolism?
faqs.answer: As little as 50 millilitres of air entering a central vein can be fatal by causing vapour lock in the right ventricle. Arterial gas embolism from diving barotrauma can occlude coronary or cerebral arteries. Immediate treatment is left lateral positioning, 100% oxygen, and hyperbaric therapy.

faqs.question: What is amniotic fluid embolism?
faqs.answer: A rare obstetric emergency in which fetal cells and amniotic fluid enter maternal circulation during labour, triggering cardiovascular collapse, severe hypoxia, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. Incidence is 1.9-7.7 per 100,000 deliveries with mortality of 20-60%.

faqs.question: What is a paradoxical embolism?
faqs.answer: Paradoxical embolism occurs when a venous clot crosses an intracardiac shunt such as a patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect into the systemic circulation, producing arterial events like stroke. It is a frequent mechanism in cryptogenic stroke in patients under 60.

faqs.question: How long do I need to take blood thinners after an embolism?
faqs.answer: Surgery-provoked VTE is treated for 3 months. Unprovoked, recurrent, or cancer-associated VTE often requires indefinite anticoagulation. Atrial fibrillation needs lifelong anticoagulation if CHA2DS2-VASc is 2 or above. Duration is reviewed at each follow-up.

faqs.question: Are DOACs better than warfarin for embolism?
faqs.answer: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are preferred over warfarin for most VTE and non-valvular atrial fibrillation. They cut intracranial bleeding roughly 50% with no INR monitoring. Warfarin stays first-line in mechanical valves and triple-positive APS.

faqs.question: Can embolism be prevented?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hospital anticoagulant prophylaxis cuts hospital-acquired VTE by 50-70%. Anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation prevents about 65% of embolic strokes. Weight control, stopping smoking, mobility during travel, and treating hypertension and diabetes reduce risk.

faqs.question: What is a septic embolism?
faqs.answer: Septic embolism is the spread of infected material, usually from bacterial vegetations on heart valves in endocarditis. Right-sided valves embolise to the lungs; left-sided valves to brain, spleen, kidneys, or limbs. Treatment combines intravenous antibiotics with valve surgery.

faqs.question: How quickly must arterial limb embolism be treated?
faqs.answer: Acute limb ischaemia from embolism must be revascularised within 6 hours to maximise limb salvage. Beyond 6 hours, irreversible muscle and nerve damage accumulates and amputation risk rises sharply. Treatment is surgical embolectomy with a Fogarty balloon catheter or catheter-directed thrombolysis.

faqs.question: Does cancer increase embolism risk?
faqs.answer: Yes. Active cancer raises VTE risk 4-7-fold through tumour procoagulant activity and chemotherapy effects. Pancreatic, gastric, brain, ovarian, and lung cancers carry the highest risk. Apixaban, edoxaban, or low molecular weight heparin are preferred for cancer-associated VTE.

faqs.question: Can pregnancy cause embolism?
faqs.answer: Pregnancy raises VTE risk 4-5-fold and up to 20-fold in the first 6 weeks postpartum from hormonal hypercoagulability, venous compression, and reduced mobility. PE remains a leading direct cause of maternal mortality. Low molecular weight heparin is the anticoagulant of choice.

faqs.question: What is chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension?
faqs.answer: CTEPH develops in 2-4% of PE survivors when residual clot organises and obstructs pulmonary arteries, causing progressive breathlessness. Pulmonary endarterectomy can cure selected patients; riociguat helps inoperable disease.

faqs.question: Should everyone with a patent foramen ovale get it closed?
faqs.answer: No. PFO is present in 25% of adults and is usually harmless. Device closure is considered in patients under 60 with cryptogenic stroke and a moderate-to-large right-to-left shunt, supported by RESPECT, CLOSE, and REDUCE trials. Decisions are joint with neurology.

faqs.question: How is embolic stroke different from haemorrhagic stroke?
faqs.answer: Embolic stroke is a clot blocking a brain artery, producing focal deficit without bleeding on initial CT. Haemorrhagic stroke is ruptured vessels with blood on CT. Treatments differ — thrombolysis and thrombectomy for embolic stroke; blood pressure control and surgery for haemorrhage.

faqs.question: Can I travel after a pulmonary embolism?
faqs.answer: Most patients can fly safely 1-2 weeks after starting anticoagulation once symptoms stabilise. On long-haul flights, walk every 1-2 hours, stay hydrated, and wear compression stockings. Patients with recent large PE or right ventricular dysfunction should consult first.

faqs.question: Is embolism hereditary?
faqs.answer: Inherited thrombophilias (factor V Leiden, prothrombin G20210A, antithrombin, protein C or S deficiency) raise VTE risk 2-50-fold. A first-degree relative with unprovoked VTE roughly doubles risk. Testing is reserved for unprovoked events in patients under 50 or for recurrence.

faqs.question: What happens if pulmonary embolism is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated PE carries 30-day mortality near 30%, mainly from right ventricular failure and recurrent embolism. Survivors face high recurrence, chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension, and lasting functional limits. Prompt anticoagulation cuts mortality below 5% in most cases.

simpleName: Embolism (blocked blood vessel from a travelling clot or other material)

regionalNames.name: रक्त के थक्के से नसों में रुकावट (Khoon ke thakke se naso mein rukawat)

metaDescription: Embolism: sudden blockage of an artery or vein by a travelling clot, fat, or air. Symptoms, CTPA diagnosis, anticoagulation, and thrombolysis explained.

images.altText: Anatomical diagram showing a pulmonary embolism with a clot lodged in a pulmonary artery after travelling from a deep vein in the leg
